title: Tests keep failing in CI but pass on my machine
body: Our CI pipeline has been randomly failing for the past few days. The test suite passes fine when I run it locally. The failures seem random — sometimes all tests pass, sometimes a handful fail. It's blocking our releases and we can't figure out what's going on.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — flaky CI failures are frustrating, especially when they block releases.

To start narrowing this down: when the failures occur, is it the same tests that fail each time, or different tests on each run? And could you share the error output or failure messages from a recent failed CI run? Even a snippet from the logs would help a lot.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Yeah it's the same tests, they're the ones from that task ordering PR we merged last week. They fail like 50% of the time. I don't have the logs in front of me right now but the test names are right there in the output, it's pretty obvious which ones. Look, they pass every single time on my machine so I don't think there's a bug in the tests themselves. Can we just figure out what's wrong with the CI environment?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's really helpful context — knowing it's specifically the tests from the task ordering PR and that they fail roughly half the time strongly suggests a race condition or environment-dependent issue.

To compare your local setup against CI, could you share some details about the CI environment? Specifically:
- What CI platform are you using (GitHub Actions, Jenkins, GitLab CI, etc.)?
- What OS and runtime versions does CI use (e.g., Node version, OS image)?
- Are there any differences you're aware of between your local setup and CI (e.g., database, parallelism settings, resource limits)?

Also, whenever you get a chance, grabbing the error output from a recent failed run would really speed things up — even just the test failure summary would be enough.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: We use GitHub Actions, Ubuntu 22.04 runners, Go 1.22. I run macOS locally with the same Go version so it should be the same thing. I don't know what other differences there are, that's kind of what I'm asking you to help figure out. The tests are straightforward ordering assertions, they work perfectly fine locally every time. I'll try to grab the logs next time it fails but honestly the failure output just shows the expected vs actual order being different, nothing exotic.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Flaky task ordering tests in CI: non-deterministic sort order causes ~50% failure rate","problem":"Tests introduced in the recent task ordering PR fail approximately 50% of the time in CI (GitHub Actions, Ubuntu 22.04) while passing consistently on the developer's local macOS machine. The failures show expected vs actual ordering mismatches.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The task ordering tests assert a specific element order that is non-deterministic — most likely due to iterating over a Go map, using unstable sort on equal elements, or relying on goroutine scheduling order. The consistent local passes on macOS are coincidental (different memory layout, scheduler behavior, or hash seed) while the Linux CI runner exposes the non-determinism. The fact that it's exactly the tests from the ordering PR and the failures show order mismatches strongly points to a missing or unstable sort key.","reproduction_steps":["Check out the branch or main after the task ordering PR was merged","Run the task ordering test suite repeatedly (e.g., `go test -count=20 ./...` filtering to the relevant package)","On Linux (or with `-race`), failures should appear within ~20 runs showing order mismatches","Alternatively, trigger the GitHub Actions CI workflow multiple times and observe ~50% failure rate"],"environment":"CI: GitHub Actions, Ubuntu 22.04, Go 1.22. Local (passing): macOS, Go 1.22.","severity":"high","impact":"Blocking releases for the team. Every CI run has a ~50% chance of spurious failure, requiring re-runs and eroding trust in the test suite.","recommended_fix":"Examine the task ordering code from the recent PR. Look for: (1) map iteration used to build ordered results without an explicit sort, (2) sort.Slice on equal elements without a tiebreaker key (Go's sort is not stable), (3) concurrent goroutines writing to a shared slice without synchronization. Add a deterministic tiebreaker to the sort (e.g., by ID or creation timestamp), or if order doesn't matter semantically, change the test assertions to be order-independent (e.g., sort both slices before comparing, or use element-set comparison).","proposed_test_case":"Run `go test -count=100` on the ordering test package and verify 100% pass rate. Additionally, add a test that explicitly constructs tasks with identical sort keys to confirm the tiebreaker produces stable ordering.","information_gaps":["Exact test names and failure output (reporter described the shape but did not paste logs)","Whether the ordering code uses map iteration, unstable sort, or concurrency internally","Whether the PR introduced new sort logic or changed existing ordering behavior"]}
